title: Ctenitis crenulans
taxonomy: Ctenitis crenulans is classified within the family Dryopteridaceae, which is a group of ferns. It is further grouped into the genus Ctenitis, a genus of ferns native to tropical and subtropical regions. The genus Ctenitis is part of the larger clade Polypodiopsida, which encompasses the majority of fern species. Ctenitis crenulans is specifically placed within the subgenus Ctenitis, where it shares characteristics with other similar fern species. This classification is based on the plant's morphological features and genetic relationships with other fern species.
uses: Ctenitis crenulans has been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat various health conditions. Its leaves are used to make infusions, which are believed to have anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antimicrobial properties. The plant is also used to treat fever, rheumatism, and digestive issues. In some cultures, Ctenitis crenulans is used to treat skin conditions, such as eczema and acne. Further studies are needed to confirm its efficacy and safety for medicinal use.
benefits: Ctenitis crenulans is a type of fern that has been traditionally used in herbal medicine. constituents: Ctenitis crenulans contains flavonoids, including quercetin, as well as terpenoids. These bioactive compounds are believed to contribute to the plant's medicinal properties. preparations: Ctenitis crenulans is a species of fern used in traditional herbal medicine. Herbal preparations of Ctenitis crenulans are typically made from the leaves and stems of the plant. intro: Ctenitis crenulans, also known as the Crenulated Wood Fern, is a species of fern native to East Asia. The plant is valued for its medicinal properties, with health benefits including anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antimicrobial effects, which may help alleviate symptoms of various conditions such as arthritis and skin infections. The bioactive constituents of Ctenitis crenulans include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and sesquiterpenes, which are responsible for its therapeutic properties. In traditional herbal medicine, the plant is often prepared as a tea or tincture, as well as being incorporated into topical creams and ointments to take advantage of its medicinal benefits.
